items.vignette: In winter there's nothing better. In summer — anything else.

items.name: Firerubin Power Armour (City Engineers' School)
items.specs: **Type:** Full plate harness with a steam drive | **Furnace:** sealed steel chamber on the back, «firerubin» mineral | **Mass:** 38 kg (with the furnace)
items.desc: An exclusive. Two or three masters in the City make these, and they will not take an order from just anyone. A plate harness with hydraulic linkages in the shoulders and thighs, on the back — a steel furnace the size of a great kettle, into which faceted pieces of the «firerubin» mineral are loaded. The mineral heats up the more tightly the space is sealed; a damper regulates the draught. The heat drives steam through copper tubes, the steam moves pistons, the pistons amplify the muscles. The wearer carries what three normally carry. The price: a constant cracking of the mineral behind the shoulder blades, the risk of fire, and the fact that any breach in the furnace is a burning rubin in the open and area burns.
items.blurb: Exclusive. Carries for three. Breach the furnace and it burns like a torch.
stats: −8 without steam, +6 with the furnace fully drawn (hydraulics assist)
+25 on strength checks while the furnace is running
furnace breach — d100/30 burns within 2 m, gambeson catches fire
≈ the price of a city block
STR 60; trained by an Engineers' School master
items.vignette: The cracking behind the shoulder blades is not fear. It is working.

items.name: Shopkeeper's Blunderbuss
items.specs: **Type:** Short muzzle-loading blunderbuss | **Calibre:** ≈ 18 mm | **Load:** shot, grapeshot, sometimes a ball | **Mass:** 3.2 kg
items.desc: What hangs above the counter of every second shop. A short barrel flared at the muzzle, a sidelock — flintlock or percussion, however it came. Loaded with whatever is at hand: shot, grapeshot, sometimes nuts and offcuts of nails. Accuracy — at a stride. But one shot can meet three night visitors at once, which matters more to the shopkeeper than accuracy. Reloading is slow and ritual: brush down the bore, measure the powder, tamp the wad, pour the shot, another wad.
items.blurb: Accurate at a stride. Generous to three. Reloading — a ritual.
stats: d100/8+4 in a 30° cone up to 5 m · d100/30+1 beyond
items.vignette: Above the counter. Under the pillow. By the door. Just in case.

items.name: Zarechye Pishchal
items.specs: **Type:** Long-barrel muzzle-loading musket | **Calibre:** ≈ 16 mm | **Lock:** matchlock or percussion | **Mass:** 5.6 kg
items.desc: Brought from Zarechye, sometimes together with the streltsy themselves. Long barrel, heavy stock, a forked rest for shooting off the wall. Hits far and hard; in a skilled hand it knocks a horseman off the saddle at twenty paces through mail. Drawbacks: mass, length, slow reload and the fact that in rain the pishchal is silent. In the City they put it on the wall and trust the seasonally hired streltsy with it, not their own — the City men's traditions are different.
items.blurb: From the wall — drops a horseman at twenty paces through mail.
items.vignette: In the rain — silent. In dry weather — visible at thirty paces.

items.name: Streletsky Berdysh
items.specs: **Type:** Streltsy crescent polearm | **Length:** 1.5 m | **Blade:** 60 cm crescent | **Mass:** 3.8 kg
items.desc: Paired to the pishchal. On the march — over the shoulder, in the line — its butt as a rest under the pishchal, in close — a polearm. The blade is a wide crescent, the edge wider than the haft itself; it cuts, slices, catches the upper rim of a shield and pulls it down. To the strelets it is both a shooter's rest and a second weapon for the case when the powder is gone, the shooter is gone, or everything is gone.
items.blurb: Both a rest and an axe. For when everything has run out.
items.vignette: Over the shoulder — an axe. Under the stock — the same one.

items.name: City Militia Arquebus
items.specs: **Type:** Medium muzzle-loading musket | **Calibre:** ≈ 14 mm | **Lock:** percussion | **Mass:** 4.4 kg
items.desc: The City militia's standard. Shorter than a pishchal, simpler than a blunderbuss, loads strictly by the regulation, fires quietly but hurts. The arquebus is issued to everyone called up for a month of defence; those who kept it after service have it hanging on the wall as a promise. In families they say that in a true crisis the arquebus comes off the wall even at grandmother's hand, and that is not a joke.
items.blurb: Militia standard. After service it hangs on the wall as a promise.
items.vignette: Grandmothers will take it off the wall. And that is not a joke.

items.name: Engineer's Revolver (break-open, swappable cylinders)
items.specs: **Type:** Six-shot revolver, heavy calibre | **Calibre:** ≈ 14 mm | **Load:** black powder + integrated caps | **Special:** break-open frame, swappable cylinders | **Mass:** 1.8 kg (frame) + 600 g (cylinder)
items.desc: A wonder of the City Engineers' School and the reason one books an appointment with Master Celeste two years in advance. The revolver is break-open — the frame breaks at the middle, like a Smith & Wesson; the cylinder comes out whole and is replaced by a spare. In this system the cylinder is the cartridge: six chambers already loaded with powder and ball, percussion caps integrated into the chambers on the hammer side. Reloading in combat = pull the empty cylinder out, slot in a new one, snap the frame shut. Three seconds against the blunderbuss ritual. The price: black powder (after the first shot it can be seen from the next district), cost (one revolver buys a condottiero's sabre and half a cuirass), and rarity — two masters, two hundred customers.
items.blurb: The cylinder is the cartridge. Six shots, swap, snap, six more.
stats: 1 cylinder (6 chambers) + 2–3 spares in the holster set
first shot — black powder cloud, −10 stealth for 30 s
items.vignette: Six. Break. New cylinder. Six. The smell of soot lingers for an hour.

items.name: Seal of House Orsini
items.specs: **Type:** Wax seal of the House head | **Material:** silver, bone | **Mass:** 80 g
items.desc: A small object that weighs more than it looks. The seal is worn on a chain at the belt or the neck; it closes envelopes, contracts and sentences. To forge it is — theoretically — possible, but in the City a forged seal is recognised by the smell of the wax and the angle of the imprint. The bearer of the seal is the voice of the House; what he says, the House has said. To take the seal off one's own neck in life is either the handover of power or treason.
items.blurb: The bearer of the seal is the voice of the House. To take it off in life — power transferred or treason.
stats: imprint identifies the House and the order
+30 TR in negotiations with a genuine seal
theft of the seal — cause for a war of Houses
items.vignette: The House's voice rests in the fist. And weighs more than anything the fist has held.

items.name: Reliquary with Founder's Ash
items.specs: **Type:** Wearable locket-box | **Material:** blackened silver | **Mass:** 140 g
items.desc: Inside — a pinch of ash. Whose, exactly — every House has its own version, and one does not cross-check. The reliquary is opened rarely, mostly before a fight — pressed to the forehead, addressed, closed. Whether it helps — no one can prove, but among those who opened the reliquary before a good fight, statistically there are fewer losers. Perhaps because the losers don't go into the fight at all.
items.blurb: Opened before a fight. Whether it helps — no one can prove.
stats: +5 TR on one action/fight when opened
no more than once per day
items.vignette: Some of those it didn't help — were never in the fight.

items.name: Borghese's Silver Mirror
items.specs: **Type:** Pocket mirror in a silver frame | **Diameter:** 70 mm | **Mass:** 95 g
items.desc: At a glance — an ordinary pocket mirror of the kind any lady of a decent House carries on a chain. But this is silver, and silver in the City is not worn for no reason. In the reflection of such a mirror one sometimes sees what is not in the room: a shadow behind the back, a figure by the door, the one who has just left and thought he left no trace. Borghese are taught these mirrors from childhood; a stranger who glances into one will, at best, see nothing.
items.blurb: Sometimes shows what is not in the room.
stats: detection of the hidden / Other presence in reflection
up to 5 m from the mirror, along the line of reflection
1 AP per use, AGL check to read it
items.vignette: Silver is not worn for nothing in the City. The mirror knows.

items.name: Chain of Forebears
items.specs: **Type:** Wearable chain with pendants | **Material:** silver, bronze, bone | **Mass:** 220–340 g
items.desc: A chain to which a single link is added for every forebear whose name is remembered. Some carry three links; the Sforza — by rumour — twenty-four. The chain is not magic in the ordinary sense; it is memory, and in the City memory is counted as power. Worn against the skin, under the shirt, never shown. If the chain is torn from the neck, the House goes to take revenge regardless of who the bearer was, how exactly he was killed, and whether he deserved it.
items.blurb: A link per forebear. If it is torn off — the House goes to take revenge.
stats: +3 TR on will checks while worn
House identification by the distinctive link work
loss/tearing — blood insult to the House
birth into or adoption by a House
items.vignette: Memory is power. The chain is the weight of memory.

items.name: Merchant's Scales
items.specs: **Type:** Equal-arm balance scales with weights | **Material:** copper, brass, bone, leather case | **Accuracy:** to 0.2 g | **Mass (set):** 1.8 kg
items.desc: Professional scales — two polished copper pans on a yoke, a thin tongue-pointer over the scale, a set of weights in a velvet socket. A respectable merchant keeps the scales out on the counter; a dishonest one keeps them in the back room in two pairs. Each weight bears the city steward's stamp — so a forgery is caught in one verification weighing. Doctored scales are a separate charge and a quick judgement.
items.blurb: Honest puts on the counter. Dishonest keeps two pairs.
stats: +10 TR on trade checks with stamped scales
forgery — steward's court, fine ×10 of shop turnover
items.vignette: The pointer dipped. The merchant did not. The buyer saw both.

items.name: Counting Abacus
items.specs: **Type:** Wooden counting frame with bead-rails | **Size:** 32 × 18 cm | **Beads:** 70 (ten rows of seven) | **Mass:** 0.9 kg
items.desc: On the counter, on the scribe's table, on the caravan steward's shelf — the abacus works where money works. Cherry-wood beads, oak frame, brass corners. An old abacus carries a black notch on one bead: the counter remembers that exactly this one once lied to him. In the City every merchant has his own counting dialect — chequer, row, his own — and at the abacus usually sits not the merchant himself but his senior clerk with twenty years and two pairs of spectacles.
items.blurb: Where money works, it works. Every merchant counts his own way.
stats: +15 TR on profit-counting; −20 TR for an auditor without his own abacus
knowledge of count; dialect — every school its own
items.vignette: A black notch on the third bead. The counter remembers.

items.name: Stamped Weights Set
items.specs: **Type:** City trade-weight set | **Material:** bronze | **Denominations:** 1 / 2 / 5 / 10 / 20 / 50 / 100 / 200 / 500 g | **Mass (set):** 0.9 kg
items.desc: Nine bronze weights in a velvet socket, each bearing the city steward's stamp and its own serial number struck on the side. A merchant with even one unstamped weight cannot trade in the City — his patent is pulled at the first check. The set itself costs a week's turnover of a good shop; losing a weight is a major trouble; finding someone else's and not returning it is the shortest path to a bad conversation.
items.blurb: Without the stamp — patent pulled. Find someone else's and don't return it — a bad talk.
stats: ≈ a week's turnover of a medium shop
items.vignette: Nine of them weigh more than the merchant. And cost more.

items.name: Workshop Stamp
items.specs: **Type:** Hot-and-cold marking stamp | **Material:** hardened steel, copper sleeve, wooden handle | **Mass:** 280 g
items.desc: A small steel stamp — the workshop's mark and the year. Struck on iron to oxide, pressed into wax, driven into sealing-wax. A master guards his stamp like his own name: forging another's stamp in the City is the charge of «insult to the craft», for which the fine is such that sometimes it is cheaper to burn down the workshop and leave. On some stamps the back side carries a hidden micro-hole, only readable under a lens; that is how an original is told from a copy.
items.blurb: Forging another's stamp — charge «insult to the craft». Fine such that leaving is cheaper.
stats: identifies the article — master, year, series
+20 TR on «this is my workshop's work» checks
hidden micro-mark — a copy reads under a lens
items.vignette: The lens will tell. The steward will judge.

items.name: Multi-coin Casket
items.specs: **Type:** Merchant's casket for varied coinage | **Material:** stained oak, brass corners, four compartments with lids | **Mass (empty):** 0.7 kg
items.desc: Four coins of the main Houses circulate in the City, plus the Zarechye hryvenak, plus the Budat dinar-crescent for travellers, plus the Gatton florin for guests. The casket sorts them into compartments, each for its coin, with its own lid and its own catch. An experienced merchant opens the right compartment by touch without looking at the customer — because looking at the customer is forbidden, the customer would then understand the price.
items.blurb: Four compartments, four catches. And you don't look at the customer.
stats: +5 TR on speed of change in foreign coin
break-in — standard lock (AGL 35 for an expert)
items.vignette: Look at the customer — and the customer understands the price.

items.name: Quill and Inkwell
items.specs: **Type:** Goose quill, horn inkwell, pen-knife | **Ink:** lampblack on oak-gall with gum arabic | **Mass (set):** 280 g
items.desc: The City's basic set. The goose quill is cut for the left or the right hand with a separate pen-knife; the horn inkwell is carried on the belt, stopped with a cork plug and a leather cap. «Oakgall» ink dries in half an hour and then no water lifts it — what is written stays. A good scribe can lift a blot with hot water and salt, but a blot where there is a contract is always grounds for suspicion.
items.blurb: What is written, stays. A blot in a contract — always grounds.
stats: the quill is cut for left/right hand — personal
one fill ≈ 4–6 sheets of fine writing
items.vignette: Half an hour to dry. Forever to remember.

items.name: Writing Stick (Budat Caliphate)
items.specs: **Type:** Exotic writing tool from the Budat Caliphate | **Construction:** half-fired clay sleeve, natural mineral crystal core | **Length:** 14 cm | **Mass:** 22 g
items.desc: Brought from the East, expensive. In construction — like a pencil, except the sleeve is not of wood but of a particular half-fired clay, thin and matte. Inside — not «lead», but a natural mineral that in the Caliphate grows in semi-transparent druses resembling quartz; the crystal from these druses writes in a fine black line and wears down by itself, like chalk. As it wears, the sleeve is broken with the fingers — the clay crumbles along a transverse score, and the crystal advances. The stick needs no ink, fears no jolt, and does not smudge on the move. City scribes complain about the price; city topographers and engineers do not — without the stick the chart will not work.
items.blurb: A pencil before pencils. Sleeve — fired clay; lead — a quartz-like mineral.
stats: +10 TR on draughting/field notes (does not smudge)
items.vignette: The chart will not smudge. The topographer sleeps better. The merchant — no.

items.name: Wax Tablets and Stylus
items.specs: **Type:** Wooden diptych tablet with a wax field + bronze stylus | **Size:** 18 × 12 cm diptych, 14 cm stylus | **Mass:** 220 g
items.desc: An old Roman habit that still lives in the City. Two hinged boards; the inner side filled with wax; you write with the sharp end of the stylus and erase with the flat end. Made a draft — wiped it with the palm, warmed it over a candle, and the field is clean again. Tablets are carried to school, to the forum, to the market — everything that does not need to be kept forever is written on them. They save the parchment, save the ink, save the nerves of whoever has to read your blot later.
items.blurb: Made a draft, wiped, warmed. The field is clean again.
stats: wax field is renewed by candle warmth
items.vignette: Anything not on parchment — not forever.

items.name: Roll of Clean Parchment
items.specs: **Type:** Calfskin parchment, roll form | **Size:** 30 cm × 4 m (standard «contract» roll) | **Grade:** I (full scrape, both sides) | **Mass:** 380 g
items.desc: Carried in a leather tube with a brass cap and a wax seal on the throat. Grade I parchment is calfskin scraped on both sides to a semi-translucent softness, without capillary pinholes and without grease stains. A contract written on such parchment will survive three generations, two wars and one fire: parchment dries but does not crumble, and reads even after a soaking. Bought from the tanners' guild on pre-order; grades II and III are cheaper but for a serious contract they will not be taken — the steward will refuse.
items.blurb: Survives three generations. Two wars. One fire.
stats: I — expensive; II — fair; III — copper
items.vignette: Dries. Does not crumble. Remembers.

items.name: Ink Stick and Brush (eastern set)
items.specs: **Type:** Black stick ink, ink-stone, soft-hair brush | **Supplier:** Budat Caliphate | **Mass:** 180 g
items.desc: Brought from the East together with the writing stick. The ink — a solid stick of resin-and-lampblack — is rubbed with water in a stone hollow. The brush is thin, of soft hair, and holds a line from hairlike to half-centimetre. Eastern scribes and the City's calligraphers work with this set: they sign ceremonial charters, draw flourishes, keep the Houses' ceremonial registers. In business correspondence ink is not used — it does not dry as fast as «oakgall», and in haste it blots.
items.blurb: Ceremonial charters, flourishes. In haste it blots; business doesn't use it.
stats: dries in 1–2 minutes — unfit for business correspondence
one stick ≈ 200 sheets of ceremonial writing
items.vignette: Oakgall for the grandfather. Ink for the grandson.
